whatItDoes: Provides a quick-reference guide to all HTTP status codes organized by category (1xx-5xx) with descriptions, common causes, and when each status code is typically returned by servers.
whoShouldUse: Web developers debugging API responses, backend engineers designing error handling, students learning HTTP protocols, and DevOps engineers troubleshooting web server issues.
whyItExists: HTTP status codes are essential for understanding API behavior, but their meanings are scattered across documentation. This tool consolidates all status codes in one searchable, categorized reference.
useCases: Look up why a 429 status code is returned and how to handle rate limiting
Understand the difference between 301 and 302 redirects for SEO purposes
Find the correct status code to return when a resource hasn't been modified (304)
Debug a 502 Bad Gateway error by understanding what causes it
Reference status codes while designing a REST API error response strategy
instructions: Browse status codes by category using the category tabs (Informational, Success, Redirection, Client Error, Server Error)
Use the search bar to find a specific status code number or keyword
Read the description and common use case for each status code
Click on any status code to see detailed information about when it's used
Use the copy button to quickly copy status code numbers for use in code
examples: Search: 'rate limit'. Output: 429 Too Many Requests — returned when a client exceeds the rate limit, with Retry-After header.
Browse: 2xx category. Output: List of all success codes (200, 201, 204, etc.) with descriptions of when to use each.
bestPractices: Use specific status codes rather than generic 200 or 500 for all responses
Return 201 Created with a Location header when creating new resources
Use 304 Not Modified with ETags to reduce bandwidth for unchanged resources
Include descriptive error messages in the response body alongside 4xx/5xx codes
commonMistakes: Returning 200 OK for error responses — use appropriate 4xx/5xx codes instead
Using 301 redirect for temporary changes — use 302 or 307 for temporary redirects
Not including a Retry-After header with 429 or 503 responses
Treating all 5xx errors the same — 500 is unexpected, 502 is gateway, 503 is overloaded
faq: What's the difference between 301 and 302 redirects? 301 is permanent (search engines update their index), 302 is temporary.
When should I use 204 No Content? Use it when the request succeeds but there's no response body, like after a successful DELETE.
Why is 418 I'm a teapot in the list? It's an April Fools joke from RFC 2324, but it is a real registered status code.
Should I return 400 or 422 for validation errors? 400 for malformed requests, 422 for well-formed requests with invalid data.
